beats.prompt: Match each game-theory term to its definition.

pairs.left: a move best no matter what the opponent does

feedback.correct: Dominant: the move beats every opponent response. Arbitrage is the same idea applied to prices — no risky step, strict gain. Markets erase it, which forces the parity identity.
hints: One card names the strictly-better move, one names the stable equilibrium.
Dominant = beats all responses; Nash = no player can profitably deviate.
dominant; Nash equilibrium.

beats.prompt: Same stock, S=100, K=95, r=0. Market quotes: C=$8, P=$2. The call costs $6 more than the put, but S−K is only $5. Is the extra $1 free money?

options: Yes — parity forces C−P to equal S−K=$5, but the quotes give $6, so there's a risk-free edge.
No — you can't call it mispriced without a volatility / Black-Scholes model.
No — C−P should equal K−S = −$5, and these quotes look close enough.

Yes — parity forces C−P to equal S−K=$5,.note: Right — the gap (C−P)−(S−K) = 6−5 = 1 is a genuine $1 arbitrage. The explore builds the conversion that harvests it, and the mastery proves it nets +$1 today, $0 at expiry (#7).

No — you can't call it mispriced without.note: Parity is an exact no-arbitrage identity, not an empirical fit. Two portfolios with the same expiry payoff must cost the same today; no σ and no Black-Scholes enter. The mismatch is real — and harvestable (#7).

No — C−P should equal K−S = −$5, and the.note: Sign check: a long call + short put pays S_T−K at expiry (it synthesizes stock-minus-strike), so C−P = S−K·D, not K−S. Verify the payoff at S_T≥K and at S_T<K (#5).

hero.structuralReadout: Scale balanced — conversion assembled, $1 gap extracted.

beats.prompt: Two-pan balance: left pan shows C + K·D, right pan shows P + S. With C=8, P=2, S=100, K=95, D=1 the left pan dips (gap = +1). Build the conversion that rebalances the scale to flat.

feedback.correct: Scale balanced — you've assembled the conversion and extracted the $1 gap.
hints: Drag or tap legs from the palette into the conversion tray.
The gap is +1 (left pan too heavy). Sell the overpriced side (call) and buy the cheap side.
Conversion: sell call (qty=−1), buy put (qty=+1), buy stock (qty=+1), borrow 95 (qty=−1). Gap → 0.

beats.prompt: Three lenses on the same no-arbitrage identity — read the card.

interaction.title: Same payoff ⇒ same price
interaction.body: Two portfolios with the same expiry payoff must cost the same today — no Black-Scholes, no σ needed. This forces the identity: C − P = S − K·D. At r=0 (D=1): C − P = S − K. No-arb bounds also follow: max(S − K·D, 0) ≤ C ≤ S. Example: S=100, K=90, D=1 → 10 ≤ C ≤ 100.

feedback.correct: Two portfolios with the same payoff cost the same — no σ, no model, just no free lunch.
hints: Read each lens: same payoff → same price; the identity C−P=S−K·D; the no-arb bounds.
C − P = S − K·D is the exact no-arbitrage identity. The bounds max(S−K·D,0) ≤ C ≤ S follow from the same principle.
Continue when ready.

beats.prompt: r=0, S=100, K=100, C=10 (identity and all inputs handed). Type the missing put price.

feedback.correct: P = C−(S−K) = 10−(100−100) = 10. No model needed — no-arbitrage alone pins the put price.
hints: The parity identity is C−P = S−K at r=0. You have C, S, K — rearrange for P.
P = 10 (#6). S=K so the gap term vanishes — the put and call cost the same.

beats.prompt: You're quoted three of {call price, put price, spot price, discounted strike}. Which move prices the missing one fastest?

Put–call parity.note: One identity ties C, P, S and K·D; rearranging for the missing quote is a single step. Fastest path — you don't rebuild anything.

No-arbitrage replication.note: You could rebuild the replicating portfolio leg-by-leg from scratch — but the parity identity already encodes that replication. Recognizing the identity is the faster move; replication is the long path to the same number.

Payoff construction.note: Drawing the payoff diagram gives the value at expiry, not the price relationship today. Parity is the current-cost identity that pins the fourth quote — a payoff read alone won't.

beats.prompt: Two parity surfaces, one method. Match each problem to its answer, then name what they share.

feedback.correct: One identity C−P=S−K·D, two rearrangements. The schemaId is put-call-parity either way.
hints: Both surfaces share the same four quantities — C, P, S, K·D. What single equation connects them?
P = C−(S−K·D); C = P+(S−K·D). Same identity flipped.
One identity, two directions — solve-for-P and solve-for-C are the same method.

beats.prompt: Transfer: D=10/11, S=50, K=44, P=3. Find the no-arbitrage call price by parity. (K·D = 40 exactly.)

interaction.scenario: D=10/11 (discount bites here — not r=0). S=50, K=44, P=3. Solve C = P + S − K·D. Compute K·D first.

feedback.correct: C = P + S − K·D = 3 + 50 − 40 = 13. The discount bites now (D=10/11 ≠ 1), and K·D = 44·(10/11) = 40 exact.
hints: Same identity, but r≠0 this time: C = P + S − K·D. Compute K·D first.
K·D = 44 × (10/11) = 440/11 = 40 exactly (exact rational, no float). So C = 3 + 50 − 40.

beats.prompt: Mastery — two parts. Part A: S=100, K=95, C=8, P=2, r=0 — what is the arbitrage gap? Part B: state the conversion cash flows — profit today and net at expiry.

interaction.scenario: Part A: gap = (C−P) − (S−K). Part B: sell call, buy put, buy stock, borrow K=95. State the cash flows today and at expiry.

feedback.correct: Part A: gap = (C−P)−(S−K) = (8−2)−(100−95) = 6−5 = 1. Part B: sell call (+8), buy put (−2), buy stock (−100), borrow 95 (+95) → +$1 today; the (stock+put−call) book is always worth K=95 at expiry, repay 95 → $0 net.
hints: Part A: gap = (C−P)−(S−K). C−P = 6, S−K = 5. Part B: the scale tips toward the call — sell the overpriced side, buy the cheap side.
Part A: 6−5 = 1. Part B: sell call (+8), buy put (−2), buy stock (−100), borrow 95 (+95); sum today = +1.
Part A: gap = 1. Part B: today +$1, at expiry $0 (#7).

beats.interviewNote: Interview framing: 'Put-call parity' is a quant interview classic. The key: C − P = S − K·D (no σ, no Black-Scholes); at K=95, C=8, P=2, S=100, the gap = 1 is a real $1 arbitrage — harvest it by selling the call, buying the put, buying the stock, borrowing K·D=95 (profit today +$1, at expiry $0). No-arb bounds max(S−K·D,0) ≤ C ≤ S also follow from the same identity; at S=100, K=90: 10 ≤ C ≤ 100.

feedback.correct: Two portfolios → one price → C−P=S−K·D; the gap is the arb; the conversion (sell call, buy put, buy stock, borrow K·D) extracts it; no-arb bounds max(S−K·D,0) ≤ C ≤ S. Next: spreads and straddles compose legs.
hints: Read each recap card.
The identity, the gap, the conversion, the bounds — one connected toolkit.
Tap to finish.
